title: Flat slab wall hanging in air-dry clay
subtitle: A textured clay tile with a loop for hanging
excerpt: A flat slab wall hanging is a single piece of clay rolled flat, given texture or decoration, pierced with a hanging loop, and sealed once bone-dry. It requires less assembly than a three-dimensional form and is a good starting point for working with surface textures, impressions, and slab techniques. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Slab-built plaque technique. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: wall hanging is the most accessible pottery project: one piece of clay, rolled flat, decorated, pierced, and dried. No joins, no coil work, no assembly. The surface can be textured with stamps, pressed leaves, carved lines, or coloured slips. No kiln or wheel is needed.

attrs.body: Air-dry clay slabs warp if they dry on a surface that does not support them evenly. Dry the slab between two wooden boards, weighted lightly, to keep it flat. Check after the first hour and reweight if the edges are lifting.
attrs.title: Drying flat

items.name: Pottery rolling pin and 8 mm guide rails
Stamps, leaves, or lace for texture
Thin cord or twine for hanging

content.text: Step 1: Roll and cut the slab

Roll the clay to 8 mm thick. Cut the shape: a rectangle, a circle, an oval, or an irregular organic form. A clean fettling-knife cut gives sharper edges than tearing. For a rectangular hanging, 15 x 20 cm is a good working size.

Immediately after cutting, press textures into the surface while the clay is still soft. Press a leaf face-down with firm even pressure; the leaf veins transfer as a raised pattern. Press stamps with a single firm push rather than rocking. Lay a piece of lace flat and roll over it once with a rolling pin. Remove the texturing objects before moving to the next step.

Use the needle tool to pierce two holes near the top of the slab, about 3 cm apart and 1.5 cm from the top edge. Pierce straight through in one movement. The holes will shrink slightly as the clay dries, so make them slightly larger than the cord diameter. Thread the cord through after bone-dry and tie a loop at the back.
Place the slab on a canvas-covered board. Lay a second board on top, weighted with a book. Leave for the first four hours, then remove the top board and allow to finish drying in open air. Full

Sand any rough edges lightly with 120-grit sandpaper. Seal the front, edges, and back with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1 with water. Thread cord through the holes and tie. For a decorative finish, paint first with acrylics, then seal.
Cut letters or words into the slab with the fettling knife for a personalised piece.
Make a set of three different-sized ovals for a grouped wall installation.
Use a cookie cutter to cut a specific shape: star, moon, leaf.

attrs.intro: Issues with flat slab wall hangings.

items.fix: Dry between boards for the first day. Weight any lifting edges. If already warped, moisten the convex surface slightly and re-press between boards.
Pierce holes at least 1.5 cm from any edge. Make holes 2-3 mm larger than the cord diameter to allow for shrinkage.
items.cause: Holes pierced too close to the edge or too small.
items.symptom: Hanging holes crack around the edges

attrs.heading: Common problems
